coredata.dc:description: Heavy metal ions (HMIs) have been widely applied in various industries because of their excellent physicochemical properties. However, their discharging without appropriate treatment brought about serious pollution problems. So it is desirable but challenging to rapidly and completely clean up these toxic pollutants from water, especially utilizing environmentally friendly and naturally rich biomass materials. In this work, we prepared nanocellulose/carbon dots/magnesium hydroxide (CCMg) ternary composite using cotton via a simple hydrothermal method. The removal mechanism towards Cd2+ and Cu2+ was investigated using a combination of experimental techniques and density functional theory calculations. CCMg shows a good ability to remove HMIs. It is realized that the interaction between each component of CCMg and cadmium nitrate is mainly of hydrogen/dative bonds. Cadmium nitrate is preferentially enriched by the Mg(OH)2 moiety, proved by calculated thermodynamics, interfacial interactions and charges. After transformation, the cadmium carbonate precipitate is fixed on the surface by nanocellulose (NC) via chemical coupling; and of interest is that copper ion precipitates in the form of basic sulfate. Due to its high adsorption effect and simple recovery operation, CCMg is having a wide range of application prospects as a water treatment agent.

Optimized structures of various isomers of the nanocellulose-basic copper sulfate, labelled as NC-CuSO_n (n = a ∼ f).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metal ions (HMIs) have been widely applied in various industries because of their excellent physicochemical properties. However, their discharging without appropriate treatment brought about serious pollution problems. So it is desirable but challenging to rapidly and completely clean up these toxic pollutants from water, especially utilizing environmentally friendly and naturally rich biomass materials. In this work, we prepared nanocellulose/carbon dots/magnesium hydroxide (CCMg) ternary composite using cotton via a simple hydrothermal method. The removal mechanism towards Cd2+ and Cu2+ was investigated using a combination of experimental techniques and density functional theory calculations. CCMg shows a good ability to remove HMIs. It is realized that the interaction between each component of CCMg and cadmium nitrate is mainly of hydrogen/dative bonds. Cadmium nitrate is preferentially enriched by the Mg(OH)2 moiety, proved by calculated thermodynamics, interfacial interactions and charges. After transformation, the cadmium carbonate precipitate is fixed on the surface by nanocellulose (NC) via chemical coupling; and of interest is that copper ion precipitates in the form of basic sulfate. Due to its high adsorption effect and simple recovery operation, CCMg is having a wide range of application prospects as a water treatment agent.

An applicable CCMg water treatment agent is easily synthesized. It starts Cd2+ and Cu2+ HMIs removal by electrostatic attraction, and the resultant complexes are stabilized by electron transfer. The Mg(OH)2 component enriches HMI the most preferentially from energetic and bonding perspectives. The produced precipitates are identified as forms of carbonate and basic sulfate, and cleaned-up by NC via hydrogen and dative bonds.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data will be available on request, and most of them have been given in Supplementary Materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their physical and chemical properties [1,2], heavy metal ions (HMIs) are widely used in pigment, battery, automobile fuel, coating materials and iron and steel industry [3,4]. Although HMIs endow these materials excellent properties [5,6], they also bring serious environmental pollution problems in the cases of unsuitable treatment [7,8]. Therefore, effectively separating heavy metal ions from the environment become an important issue to scientists [9–14]. Many methods, such as chemical precipitation method [15–18], ion-exchange methods [19], adsorption methods [20,21] and membrane separation techniques [22,23] have been adopted to solve this problem [24–26]. Though most of these methods can remove HMIs efficiently [27], the adsorbed HMIs are still remained in water body as solid particles [28]. How to entirely eliminate HMIs from water and finely recover them as available source is still an open issue.

Various strategies were applied to solve this problem. Combination with magnetic particles or certain substrate that can immobilize the recovered HMIs would achieve the complete separation and recycling of HMIs [29,30]. As a natural material, cellulose has been widely studied because of its renewable, abundant and non-toxic characteristics [31–33]. Macromolecules of cellulose contain a large number of hydrogen bonds [34–36], which are able to interact with HMIs at the interface, thus achieving chemically coupled adsorption under meticulous operation [37–39]. In addition, cellulose also bears a large number of hydroxyl groups, which are easily compounded with various materials to fabricate multifunctionalized composites [40,41]. For example, inorganic materials Mg(OH)2, ZnO and CaCO3 have been combined with cellulose and its derivatives. The prepared composites were applied as water treatment agents [42,43]. These inorganic materials have good removal property for HMIs. Once combined with cellulose, the recovered HMIs-contained solids could be gathered by cellulose from water. It totally fulfills the entire removal of HMIs from water body [44,45].

Although it has been found in previous studies [42,46,47] that the cellulose can immobilize HMIs-contained salts, the interaction nature between them is hardly explained in detail. What is its driving force? What kind of interaction has been established? How does this interaction fix HMIs on cellulose? In addition, only the high concentration solution was studied in the previous work [48], but what kind of performance does the composite have in the low concentration solution that is more practical in the real environment?

In this work, a theoretical calculation associated with experiment on these questions was fully conducted. Since Mg(OH)2 has clearly identified layer structure and good adsorption/immobilization property, the research system is to choose it combining with nanocellulose (NC). Moreover, some peripheric parts of NC is fine-tuned to produce carbon dots (CDs) by the hydrothermal synthesis. Thus NC/CDs/Mg(OH)2 is finally attained, abbreviated as CCMg. Comprehensive density functional theory (DFT) calculations in terms of structure, bonding, charge and energy of CCMg with HMIs (Cd2+/Cu2+) were performed to reveal the nature and strength of interfacial interaction with assistance with relevant experiments. The results would be helpful to develop new technology to separate and recover HMIs by cellulose-based material as water treatment agent.

The chemicals used in this experiment were not re-purified and used directly. Degreased cotton was used as the source of cellulose. H2SO4, MgSO4·7H2O and NaOH were purchased from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Company. Cd(NO3)2·4H2O and CuSO4·5H2O were bought from Aladdin Co. Ltd..

CCMg was synthesized as previously reported [48]. Firstly, nanocellulose (NC) was prepared by string 1.0 g cotton in 20 mL 64 % H2SO4 at 45 °C. After 2 h reaction, NC solution was obtained by adding 20 mL deionized water. The resulting NC was washed by centrifuge until it is neutral. NC was mixed with 3.0 g MgSO4·7H2O in 50 mL deionized water. Then the pH value of the solution was adjusted to 12 by 5 mol L−1 NaOH solution. After string for 30 min, the mixture was moved to the reactor vessel and heated at 100 °C for 4 h. Later on, the product of CCMg was separated and washed by centrifugation.

0.1 g CCMg composite material was put into 400 mL solution of 20 mg L−1 Cd(NO3)2 or CuSO4. The solution was sampled at different time and tested by atomic absorption spectrometry. To consider the ion interference, KCl, NaCl, CaCl2 and MgCl2 were chosen in concentrations of 10, 50, 100 and 200 mg L−1, respectively. The HMI-recovered sample was named as CCMg-M (M = Cd and Cu). We used solutions of 50 mg L−1 M(NO3)2 (M = Cu, Cd, Zn, Ni, Ca and Mg) for the selectivity towards different heavy metal ions.

The structure of sample was analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using D/max-RC type X-ray Diffractometer produced by Rigaku, Japan. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. The morphology of sample was observed by FE-SEM (S-4800, Hitachi, Ltd., Chiyoda-ku, Japan). The chemical compositions were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha, Thermo fisher Scientific Company, US). The concentration of metal ion was determined by atomic absorption spectrometer (PerkinElmer PinAAcle 900 T, United states) and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (ICP-MS, PerkinElmer NEXION 350D, United states).

Composite components and their complexes were fully calculated at the relativistic DFT level. The three components, nanocellulose, carbon dots and magnesium hydroxide were abbreviated as NC, CDs and MH, respectively. The complexes formed by each component with the hydrated cadmium nitrate, Cd(NO3)2·H2O, were labelled as NC/CDs/MH-CdNO. Two additional forms of cadmium, single Cd2+ ion and water saturated Cd2+, were also considered, giving complexes of NC/CDs/MH-Cd2+ and -[Cd(H2O)6]2+. The recovered samples were represented by complexes of nanocellulose‑cadmium carbonate (denoted as NC-CdCO) and nanocellulose-basic copper sulfate (NC-CuSO).

Geometry structures were optimized using a quantum-chemistry Priroda code [49,50]. Unless otherwise noted, no re-optimization was conducted. We did not adopt symmetry for studied cluster systems. In Priroda, the scalar relativistic four-component all-electron (AE) Hamiltonian was exploited without considering spin-orbit coupling effects. Under the generalized gradient approximation (GGA), the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) [51] functional was employed together with correlation-consistent Gaussian basis sets [49]. Analytical frequency calculation at the optimized geometry was performed. The case without imaginary frequency indicates the minimum point at the potential energy surface, and confirms the optimized structure energetically stable. Meantime, the thermodynamic data at 298 K and Mulliken charges were acquired.

The interfacial interaction in complexes was calculated with the energy decomposition analysis (EDA). It is expressed as the total bonding energy (ΔETBE) in ADF2014 code [52–54]. The interfacial interaction energy can be decomposed as follows:
(1)

E Electro

E Disp
(2)
ΔE TEB

E Steric

E Orbit
+
E Disp
Sub-terms of EElectro, EPauli, EOrbit, EDisp and ESteric are energies of electrostatic interaction, Pauli repulsion, orbital attraction, dispersion interaction and steric interaction, respectively. EDisp was considered with the Grimme's D3 correction [55]. More details refer to the literature [56]. In all the ADF calculations, the integration parameter of 6.0 was used along with default convergence criteria. We applied scalar relativistic Hamiltonian (Zeroth-order regular approximation, ZORA) [57–59], GGA-PBE functional and Slater-type TZP basis sets.

Additionally, the interfacial interaction was also explored in light of bondings using the quantum theory of atoms in molecule (QTAIM). A single-point calculation was firstly accomplished with Gaussian 09 code [49]. The same GGA-PBE functional was used. Mixed basis sets were employed: all-electron ones of 6-31G(d, p) for S, O, N, C, H and Mg. With Stuttgart relativistic small-core effective core potentials, associated basis sets were used for Cd and Cu. Fed with the Gaussian checkpoint, MultiWFN3.6 code was used to obtain QTAIM parameters and relevant data at the interfacial bond critical points (BCP).

According to SEM in Fig. 1a, the surface of NC was fully covered by Mg(OH)2 flakes. These flakes are about 100 nm wide and 10 nm thick. After capture of HMIs, the SEM images of resultant samples vary. There are nanowires and particles on the CCMg surface instead of flakes from Fig. 1b and c [60,61]. Element mapping analyses by EDS (Fig. 1d and e) give the evidence that Cd2+ and Cu2+ have been immobilized on NC [62].

To recognize phases of Cd2+ and Cu2+ on NC, the crystalline structures of recovered CCMg-Cd/Cu were studied by XRD and results are given in Fig. 1f and g. It is shown that all samples have diffractions peaks at 14.6o, 16.3o and 22.6o, which are attributed to the typical diffractions of NC substrate (JCPDS# 50–2241) [63,64]. No Mg(OH)2 and CDs-related diffraction peaks are found in the XRD patterns of the recovered samples. Meanwhile, typical diffractions of CdCO3 and Cu4(SO4)(OH)6 are identified for CCMg-Cd and Cu, respectively. This results indicate that it is CdCO3 and Cu4(SO4)(OH)6 that are formed. They have intimate interaction with NC.

Although NC also exists in the recovered sample, its diffraction peak is difficult to observe. Because the most intensive peak of (002) plane is located at 2θ of 22.6o [63], it would be masked by the stronger peaks of (012) plane of CdCO3 and (220) plane of Cu4(SO4)(OH)6 which appear in exactly the same region. Meanwhile, there would be no peaks of Mg(OH)2 for CCMg-Cd/Cu, if the component was fully consumed out.

FT-IR of CCMg-Cd/Cu were also measured in Fig. 1h. Vibrational peaks of CO were observed in all the spectra. The peak of bare cellulose NC appears at 1026 cm−1, and it shifts to around 1167 and 1117 cm−1 for CCMg and CCMg-Cd/Cu. This implies that NC has chemical bonds with other components (CDs and Mg(OH)2) or the recovered HMI precipitates [65–67].

XPS characterizations of CCMg, CCMg-Cd and CCMg-Cu have been performed, in Fig. 2 . For the survey of CCMg (Fig. 2a), only three elements C, O and Mg are found. After adsorption, elements of Cd and Cu appear in CCMg-Cd and CCMg-Cu, respectively. This indicates that Cd2+ and Cu2+ have been up-taken by CCMg. Four peaks are detected in the Cu 2p spectrum of Fig. 2b. Peaks located at 934.6 and 954.6 eV are the binding energies of Cu 2p5/2 and 2p3/2 of CuO, and ones at 943.8 and 963.8 eV are satellite peaks of Cu 2p. To the Cd 3d XPS spectrum (Fig. 2c), one can observe a doublet peak located at 405.2 and 412.1 eV for Cd 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively [68–70]. These results further prove that Cd2+ and Cu2+ have been immobilized onto NC during up-taken process. The binding energies of C, O and Mg of above samples are listed in Table S1. It can be found that they have similar chemical environments in materials before and after HMI adsorption.

To test the content of each component in the composite, the sample CCMg was calcined at 500 °C in air condition. The mass of CCMg before and after calcination was weighed. Since the carbon dots (CDs) is zero dimensional material, its weight can be neglected [71,72]. The contents of Mg(OH)2 (MH) and nanocellulose (NC) were calculated to be 41.3 % and 58.7 %, respectively, according to the following equations.

Terms wMH and wNC are the relative percentages of Mg(OH)2 and nanocellulose in the composite, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization effect of CCMg towards HMIs
ce:section.ce:para: The immobilization effect of CCMg was studied by 20 mg L−1 Cd2+/Cu2+ solution, which is environmentally practical and 50 times lower concentration than the one in previous work [48]. The capture of HMIs by CCMg is very fast as seen in Figs. 3a. The adsorption efficiency reaches 90 % within 2 h and almost 100 % over 4 h. This evidences that recovered Cu2+ and Cd2+ salts are immobilized by NC via chemical bonding. The removal capacity with different initial concentrations were also performed. It can be seen from Fig. 3b that CCMg has high removal capacities, getting to 988.4 and 656.3 mg g−1 for Cd2+ and Cu2+, respectively. Previous relevant reports and ours were collected and summarized in Table 1 . One can note that Mg(OH)2/MgO-derived composites have relatively high removal performance, which follow the same mechanism. The removal capacity of CCMg in the work is lower than ones of some adsorbents [17,45,73]. This is because NC works as substrate. It accounts for almost 60 % mass in the CCMg composite, but is lack of removal property. This results in lower removal capacity of CCMg than of CDs/Mg(OH)2 and TOB@MgO. However, NC does have the advantage of immobilizing HMIs onto its surface, which would avoid the risk of the second pollution and make the recovery easier and more convenient.

The recovered CCMg-Cd and CCMg-Cu were treated by 0.1 M HNO3, as well as the desorbed HMIs in solutions. It is found that desorption efficiencies are about 97 % of absorbed ones in Fig. 3c. This also suggests that HMIs have been fully captured by NC via chemical interfacial interaction.

The selectivity adsorption experiments were performed on a system containing HMIs of Cu2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Ca2+ and Mg2+. The concentration of each cation was 50 mg L−1 and the total volume is 200 mL. 0.1 g CCMg was added. The supernate was sampled and tested with ICP-MS after 12 h adsorption. As seen from Fig. 3d, the removal efficiency is nearly 100 % for Cu2+, and 10–20 % for Cd2+, Zn2+ and Ni2+. There is almost no removal effect on Ca2+ and Mg2+. These results indicate that CCMg has high selectivity in removing Cu2+ when multiple HMIs co-exist.

Positively charged ions may disturb the adsorption process. Thus, the capture of HMIs experiments with interference ions of K+, Ca2+, Na+ and Mg2+ were carried out in Fig. 3e. It is deduced that positive charged ions would be adsorbed by the composite. They occupy the surface of CCMg and hinder the absorption of targeted HMIs. However, results of interfering ions experiments show no impact on Cd2+ capture by either mono or bivalent ions with concentrations from 10 to 200 mg L−1. So HMIs capture is slightly affected by existence of cations around composite, but the transformation reaction of HMIs and the interactions built between the composite and CdCO3/Cu4SO4(OH)6 play a more important role.

To test the durability of CCMg, cyclic experiments were carried out by adding 0.1 g CCMg to 400 mL 20 mg L−1 Cd2+/Cu2+ solutions. Every 3 h, solutions were replaced by fresh ones. As shown in Fig. 3f, the removal efficiencies maintain 99.4 % (Cd2+) and 92.8 % (Cu2+) even after 10 cycles. It is evident that CCMg has good durability performance and long serve life.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DFT calculations on structures and interfacial interactions
ce:section.ce:para: To supplement experimental findings and rationalize HMI removal-enhanced mechanism, we firstly embarked on optimizations of complexes that contain each component of CCMg and cadmium nitrate. See the obtainable isomers in Figs. S1 ∼ S3 and formation reaction energies in Table S2. Driven by electrostatic attractions, all components of the ternary composite CCMg are able to adsorb cadmium nitrate as summarized in Fig. 4 . Structural analyses show that hydrogen bonds and/or dative bonds would provide the interfacial coupling interaction. Consequently, reaction free energy (ΔrG) that MH component captures cadmium nitrate is the most negative, with an average of −0.63 eV for the four isomers. It is followed by CDs (−0.41 eV), and NC is the most positive (−0.28 eV). Thus, metal ions preferentially interact with Mg(OH)2 in the process of adsorption, and CDs and NC also have the enrichment effects. This is consistent with the adsorption experiment analyses.

In the aqueous solution, Cd(NO3)2 can ionize into Cd2+ and NO3 −. Besides existing as a single Cd2+ ion, it could be saturated by multiple waters to form [Cd(H2O)n]2+. So we further optimized possibly formed complexes, i.e. CCMg component with Cd2+ and with [Cd(H2O)6]2+. Energy-stable structures were illustrated in Fig. S4. Comparison of Tables S2 and S3 finds that the formation reaction energies with Cd2+/[Cd(H2O)6]2+ are more negative than ones with Cd(NO3)2. It is understandable that the component captures positively charged cadmium species more easily. Notably, the MH component has the strongest adsorption ability towards Cd species, regardless of what kind of form it is.

Experimentally, both NC and HMI precipitate appear in the recovered sample CCMg-M (M = Cd and Cu); and they are best described as NC-CdCO3 and NC-Cu4(SO4)(OH)6. DFT optimizations were performed on their molecular cluster models, marked as NC-CdCO and NC-CuSO, respectively. Seven and six isomers had been accessed to in Figs. 5 and 6
.

From Table S4 that the formation of these isomers is an exothermic process. It is feasible that cadmium and copper precipitates are gathered and immobilized by NC. Among seven isomers of NC-CdCO, it releases the most energy when forming NC-CdCO_a. It corresponds to −1.13 eV ΔrE (total energy), −1.06 eV ΔrE0 (further inclusion of zero-point vibrational energy), −1.04 eV ΔrH (enthalpy) and − 0.51 eV ΔrG (free energy). Regarding the six isomers of NC-CuSO, NC-CuSO_a has the most negative reaction energies, being −0.90, −0.85, −0.85 and − 0.28 eV, respectively.

In terms of energy decomposition, the EDA technique was used to examine interfacial interaction in above complexes. From Table 2 , the computed ΔETBE of NC-CdCO is −1.52 eV, where “a” marking the energy-lowest isomer is omitted for convenient expression. It is the sum of the sub-terms ESteric (−0.06 eV), EOrbit (−1.21 eV) and EDisp (−0.25 eV). The first one contains EElectro of −2.26 eV and EPauli of 2.19 eV. EOrbit, EElectro, and EDisp are generally considered as contributions of covalent, ionic, and weak interactions to a bond, respectively. Thus, the interfacial coupling is dominated by electrostatic interaction (60.9 %). Orbital attraction of 32.5 % indicates the existence of covalency. Weak interaction part is very small, only having 6.6 % contribution. There are two hydroxyl groups at the interface from structural optimization, which suggests the possibility of the presentence of hydrogen bonds. In addition, short interfacial OCd distance was measured, and a dative bond probably formed. The same conclusion can be drawn for NC-CuSO that has ΔETBE of −1.54 eV.

From a point of view of EDA, the component MH shows the strongest chemical interaction with HMIs, giving ΔETBE of −2.40 eV for MH-CdNO (Table 2). More positive ΔETBE values were calculated for CDs-CdNO (−1.56 eV) and NC-CdNO (−1.85 eV). Like what is indicated for NC-CdCO and NC-CuSO, the chemical interfacial coupling in MH/NC/CDs-CdNO consists of both hydrogen bonds and dative bonds, where electrostatic and orbital attraction are predominant.

The nature and strength of interfacial bonding is identified by QTAIM topological analyses [88,89]. In Table 3 , data at interfacial BCPs are presented for five complexes. The results show that all the H(r) values approach 0 and most of them are negative. Negative Eint value also indicates a chemical coupling. The calculated value of -G(r)/V(r) is approximately equal to 1.0, showing considerable unstable (kinetic energy) and stable (potential) interactions in terms of energy density. In general, ρ greater than 0.20 a.u. and ∇2ρ(r) less than 0 indicates the typical covalent bond. In contrast, ρ is less than 0.10 a.u., and ∇2ρ(r) greater than 0 behaves as a non-covalent bond. In these complexes, the interfacial interaction is attributed to hydrogen bond and/or dative bond, associated with EDA, thermodynamics and structural optimizations.

We further calculated Mulliken charges of fragments in various isomeric complexes in Table S5. The ability of CCMg that bonds cadmium nitrate depends on the distribution and magnitude of charge. In the formed complexes, the three components CDs, MH and NC of CCMg have positive charges, while the adsorbate part carries negative charge. The electron, transferred to the adsorbed cadmium nitrate, strengthens the interfacial interaction. Moreover, the charge on MH (0.112–0.244) is greater than ones on CDs (0.053–0.168) and NC (0.112–0.205). This agrees with the results that MH-CdNO has more negative reactions energies, ΔETBE and total Eint than CDs-CdNO and NC-CdNO do. It proves that Cd2+ is preferentially enriched around Mg(OH)2. Similarly, the electron transfer from NC to HMI salts has stabilized resultant complexes, which improves the immobilization of HMI precipitates.

According to experimental results, Mg(OH)2 would release OH−, which surround nanocellulose (NC). It will produce CO3 2− with the dissolved CO2 from the air. Both CDs and NC in composite are negatively charged [90,91], which adsorb and enrich positively charged Cd2+/Cu2+. By this effect, a microenvironment with OH− and Cd2+/Cu2+ around NC was built. It provides a facilitative condition for precipitating and recovering HMIs by NC.

Evidence is further given by theoretical calculations. Comparing the interaction of three components of CCMg with the cadmium species, regardless of in forms of Cd(NO3)2·H2O, Cd2+ or [Cd(H2O)6]2+, it is the strongest for the component Mg(OH)2. The interaction corresponds to the most negative reaction energies as seen in Table S2 and S3. It is shown that Mg(OH)2 can rapidly react with heavy metal ions via the intimate interaction. Consequently, the metal precipitates are immobilized by NC, proved by the computed negative reaction energies of NC-CdCO and NC-CuSO (Table S4). Moreover, the removal mechanism is further rationalized by calculations of EDA, QTAIM and charges.
In brief, the capture process may involve 3 steps: adsorption, transformation reaction and immobilization, which are all dominated by interfacial interactions as shown in Fig. 7 .

CCMg was prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. Adsorption experiments associated with a series of characterizations were carried out. And theoretical calculations and analyses were conducted from the aspects of structure and interfacial interaction. It is shown that the surface of NC is completely covered by Mg(OH)2 thin sheet. To start HMI removal, each CCMg component can hold cadmium nitrate via hydrogen bonds and/or dative bonds. Of those, the interaction with Mg(OH)2 is the strongest, with the largest thermodynamic reaction energy (−1.52 eV), interfacial interaction energies (−1.85 ∼ −2.40 eV) and electron transfer (0.24). The prepared CCMg composite material effectively removes Cd2+ and Cu2+ from wastewater. Their precipitates appear in forms of carbonate and basic sulfate, respectively, and are immobilized by NC via chemical coupling. It is expected that our composite has a promising application as a water treatment agent.

Despite having many merits, the water treatment CCMg has some limitations. Its mechanical strength is slightly reduced with respect to the cellulose substrate. The ion exchange with HMI leads to the release of Mg2+ to the water. Further experimentation like modifying cellulose with certain functional group to complex Mg2+ is ongoing.
